title: Quiz: why cache, and what problem does it introduce?
prompt_md: Why do backends add a cache (e.g. Redis) in front of the database, and what is the main problem caching introduces?
options: Caching is only for storing user passwords securely, and the problem is encryption overhead
Caching makes the database optional; the problem is it uses more disk
Caching reduces load, latency, and cost by serving hot data from memory, but it risks serving stale data — so the hard part is cache invalidation
Caching guarantees the data is always perfectly up to date; there is no real downside
explanation_md: A cache stores hot data in fast memory, which **reduces database load, lowers latency, and cuts cost** by avoiding repeated expensive queries. The catch is that the cached copy can drift from the source of truth — it becomes **stale** — which is why **cache invalidation** (deciding when to update or evict an entry) is famously one of the two hard problems in computing. You manage it with strategies like **TTL** (time-to-live expiry), write-through, or explicit invalidation on update. Accept the tradeoff: you're trading a small window of staleness (**eventual consistency**) for big performance wins.
